---
title: "Confidence Intervals: What \"95% Confident\" Actually Means"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240701)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # samples / point estimates
TRUTH  = "#c1121f"     # the fixed parameter / limiting object
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # intervals that cover
MISS   = "#d6791f"     # intervals that miss
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas

def normal_pdf(t, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The Gaussian density — used to draw the pivot and the tail areas."""
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This opens the **Interval Estimation** movement (C) of
> the Statistical Inference track. It assumes two earlier articles. From [INFR-A1 *The
> Estimator as a Random Variable*] it takes the central idea that an estimator
> $\hat\theta$ is a *random variable* with a **sampling distribution**, whose standard
> deviation is the **standard error** $\mathrm{SE}=\sigma/\sqrt n$. From [INFR-A2 *LLN and
> the CLT*] it takes the **Central Limit Theorem** — that the standardized sample mean
> $(\bar X_n-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ converges in distribution to a standard Gaussian. The
> only thing imported from outside the track is the existence and value of the **standard
> normal quantiles** $z_{\alpha/2}$ (e.g. $z_{0.025}=1.96$), built in the Probability
> Foundations track [PROB-11]; we use them here only by name. This article installs the
> *concept* of interval estimation — what a confidence interval claims, the pivotal-quantity
> machine that constructs one, and the misinterpretations that sink most people in an exam
> or interview. It **sets up** [INFR-C2 *The t-Distribution and Small-Sample Intervals*]
> (what to do when $\sigma$ is unknown), [INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]
> (intervals for any MLE), the bootstrap interval [INFR-E2], and the deliberate contrast
> with the Bayesian **credible interval** [INFR-E4].

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A point estimate is a single number with no honesty about its own error; a **confidence
interval** is the honest version — a *random interval* engineered so that, across the
many samples we might have drawn, a fixed fraction of them trap the true parameter. This
article builds that idea from the ground up. We define **coverage** carefully — the
interval is random, the parameter is fixed, and "$95\%$" is a property of the *procedure*,
not of the one interval in your hands. We derive the general **pivotal-quantity** method,
the single machine that manufactures intervals, and run it end-to-end on the canonical
case: a confidence interval for a Normal mean with known variance, $\bar X \pm
z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. We verify coverage by simulating ten thousand parallel
universes and counting how many intervals catch the truth. Then we spend real effort on
the four classic misinterpretations — the ones an interviewer is hoping you will stumble
into — and state precisely what the interval does and does not say. The single claim the
article defends is that **the confidence is in the factory, not the widget**: a procedure
that succeeds $95\%$ of the time produces, this once, an interval that either contains the
parameter or does not, with no probability left over to attach to it.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Fix a confidence level $1-\alpha$ (e.g. $95\%$, so $\alpha=0.05$). A **confidence
interval** is a pair of statistics $\bigl(L(X_1,\dots,X_n),\,U(X_1,\dots,X_n)\bigr)$ —
*functions of the random sample*, hence themselves random — with the **coverage**
property
$$
P\bigl(L \le \theta \le U\bigr) \;=\; 1-\alpha
\qquad\text{for every possible value of the fixed parameter } \theta.
$$

Read the probability correctly: **$\theta$ is a fixed (unknown) constant; the randomness
lives entirely in $L$ and $U$**, which move from sample to sample. "$95\%$ confidence"
describes the *procedure* — repeat the experiment many times and about $95\%$ of the
intervals it produces will straddle the true $\theta$. It does **not** say the one
interval you computed has a $95\%$ chance of containing $\theta$: that interval is already
cast, and $\theta$ either lies in it or it does not.

For a Normal mean with known variance, the machine produces
$$
\bar X \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n},
$$
and the whole article is the derivation of that formula and the careful unpacking of what
the probability above means.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The single hardest thing to keep straight — and the
thing every exam and interview probes — is *where the randomness is*. Before we have data,
$L$ and $U$ are random and the coverage statement is a genuine probability. After we have
data, $L$ and $U$ are numbers, $\theta$ is a number, and the only honest statements left
are "$\theta$ is in it" or "$\theta$ is not in it." The $95\%$ never attached to $\theta$;
it attached to the *recipe*.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the interval {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **how do we report not just an estimate,
but how much to trust it — and what exactly are we promising when we do?**

INFR-A1 made the problem sharp. A point estimate like the sample mean $\bar X$ is a single
draw from a sampling distribution; collect a different sample and you get a different
number. Reporting "the mean income is \$54,300" with no qualifier is dishonest in a
specific way: it hides the fact that the estimate wobbles, and gives the reader no way to
tell a precise estimate from a noisy one. INFR-A2 then handed us the *size* and *shape* of
that wobble — the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ and the Gaussian shape from the CLT. We
now have everything we need to turn "here is my estimate" into "here is my estimate, and
here is a range I am willing to stand behind."

The naive first attempt is to slap a standard error onto the estimate: report
$\bar X \pm \mathrm{SE}$. That is the right *shape* but it leaves the crucial question
unanswered: **stand behind it how strongly?** A range of one standard error means
something different from a range of two, and until we say *what fraction of the time the
range is right*, "$\pm\,\mathrm{SE}$" is just decoration. The confidence interval is the
disciplined version: we choose the strength up front (the level $1-\alpha$), and we build
the range so that it delivers exactly that strength — not in any one use, but across the
long run of uses.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just report the standard error and let the reader decide?"* You can, and many
papers do — but the standard error alone does not tell the reader *how often* a range of a
given width is correct. That mapping from width to reliability requires knowing the shape
of the sampling distribution, which is exactly what the CLT (or an exact distributional
result) supplies. The confidence interval bundles the standard error with that shape and a
chosen reliability into one object. The point estimate answers "what is my best guess";
the standard error answers "how much does the guess wobble"; the confidence interval
answers "what range am I willing to stand behind, and how often will I be right to do so."
:::

## A two-paragraph history

The modern confidence interval is younger than most of the statistics around it. Through
the 1920s, statisticians reported estimates with "probable errors," and there was real
confusion about whether one could attach a probability to a statement about a fixed
parameter — the very confusion this article is built to dissolve. The Bayesian answer (put
a probability distribution on $\theta$ directly) existed but was contested.

In 1937 **Jerzy Neyman** cut the knot in a paper to the Royal Society. His move was to
*refuse* to put probability on $\theta$ and instead put it on the **procedure**: design an
interval-producing rule whose long-run success rate is controllable, and report that rate.
This is the frequentist confidence interval, and its defining feature — that the
confidence is a property of the method, not of any single interval — is precisely what
trips people up to this day. Neyman was explicit that the interval makes *no* probability
statement about the particular $\theta$; that distinction is the spine of everything below,
and the deliberate contrast with the Bayesian **credible interval**, which *does* put
probability on $\theta$, is taken up in [INFR-E4].

# Intuition before symbols: the dragstrip of intervals {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the picture that makes coverage click. Imagine running your
study not once but thousands of times, each time drawing a fresh sample from the same
population and computing an interval by the same recipe. The true parameter $\theta$ is a
fixed vertical line. Each study produces a horizontal interval at its own height; some
straddle the line, some sit entirely to one side and miss.

A *valid $95\%$ procedure* is one whose intervals catch the line about $95$ times in $100$
over this imagined repetition. @fig-coverage-intuition draws exactly this — fifty
intervals from fifty samples, the misses flagged. Stare at it and absorb the one lesson:
**the parameter never moves; the intervals do.** Any single interval is a frozen snapshot
— it has caught the line or it has not. The "$95\%$" is a statement about the *collection*.

```{python}
#| label: fig-coverage-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Coverage, made literal. The true mean $\mu=10$ is the fixed vertical red line. We draw 50 independent samples (each $n=25$, $\sigma=4$) and from each build the 95% interval $\bar X \pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. Each horizontal bar is one interval at its own height; green bars cover $\mu$, orange bars miss. Across many such samples the fraction that cover converges to 95%. The parameter is fixed; the intervals are the random things — that is the entire meaning of ''confidence''.'
mu_true, sigma, n = 10.0, 4.0, 25
n_intervals = 50
z = 1.96
se = sigma / np.sqrt(n)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 5.2))
n_miss = 0
for k in range(n_intervals):
    sample = rng.normal(mu_true, sigma, size=n)
    xbar = sample.mean()
    lo, hi = xbar - z * se, xbar + z * se
    covers = lo <= mu_true <= hi
    if not covers:
        n_miss += 1
    color = ACCENT if covers else MISS
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [k, k], color=color, lw=1.8, solid_capstyle="round")
    ax.plot([xbar], [k], "o", color=color, ms=3)
ax.axvline(mu_true, color=TRUTH, lw=2.0, label=fr"true mean $\mu={mu_true:.0f}$")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel(r"value")
ax.set_title(f"50 intervals, {n_miss} miss the truth (expect ~2-3 at 95%)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"After I compute one interval, say $[9.1, 11.3]$, isn't there a $95\%$ chance the mean is
in it?"* No — and this is the single most common error on the topic. Once the interval is
computed it is a fixed pair of numbers, and the mean is a fixed number; the statement
"$9.1 \le \mu \le 11.3$" is either true or false, with no probability between. The $95\%$
was spent on the *procedure* that generated the endpoints. The honest gloss is: "this
interval was produced by a method that captures the truth $95\%$ of the time." We make this
precise in @sec-misinterpret, where it is the first of four classic traps.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## What a confidence interval is, exactly {#sec-definition}

::: {#def-ci}
**Definition (confidence interval).** Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be a sample whose distribution
depends on an unknown parameter $\theta$. A **confidence interval** for $\theta$ at
**confidence level** $1-\alpha$ is a pair of statistics
$L = L(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ and $U = U(X_1,\dots,X_n)$, with $L\le U$, such that
$$
P_\theta\bigl(L \le \theta \le U\bigr) \;=\; 1-\alpha
\qquad\text{for every } \theta.
$$
The quantity $1-\alpha$ is the **coverage probability**. When the equality is replaced by
$\ge 1-\alpha$ the interval is **conservative** (it covers at least as often as advertised).
:::

Three things in this definition carry the entire weight, and we name each so no symbol is a
mystery.

- **$L$ and $U$ are statistics**, i.e. functions of the random sample. They inherit the
  sample's randomness, so they are random variables with their own distributions. This is
  the same move as in INFR-A1: a function of a random sample is random.
- **$\theta$ is a fixed constant.** It is unknown to us, but it is not random — there is a
  single true mean income, a single true defect rate. The subscript on $P_\theta$ records
  that the probability is computed *as if* $\theta$ were the true value; the coverage must
  hold whatever that value is.
- **The probability is over the randomness in $L,U$.** The event $\{L\le\theta\le U\}$ is
  random *because its endpoints are random*, not because $\theta$ is. Read the event as
  "the random interval $[L,U]$ happens to land around the fixed point $\theta$."

::: {.callout-important title="Where the randomness lives"}
In INFR-A1 the conceptual pivot was that an *estimate* is a draw from a distribution. Here
the pivot is the twin of that: an *interval* is a draw from a distribution of intervals.
Before you see data, $P(L\le\theta\le U)=0.95$ is a genuine probability statement about a
random interval. After you see data, $L$ and $U$ are numbers and the statement is just true
or false. Holding this straight is the whole game; every misinterpretation in
@sec-misinterpret is a failure to hold it.
:::

## The pivotal-quantity machine {#sec-pivot}

We need a *general* way to manufacture an $[L,U]$ with guaranteed coverage, not a lucky
guess. The standard machine is the **pivotal quantity**, and it is worth meeting in the
abstract because every interval in this track — t-intervals, Wald intervals, even the
bootstrap's pivotal interval — is the same trick.

::: {#def-pivot}
**Definition (pivotal quantity).** A **pivot** is a function
$Q = Q(X_1,\dots,X_n;\theta)$ of *both* the data and the parameter whose distribution is
**completely known** — the same for every value of $\theta$. It is allowed to contain
$\theta$; what it may not contain is any *other* unknown.
:::

The idea is almost sleight of hand, and it is worth saying slowly *why* it works. If $Q$'s
distribution is known and free of unknowns, we can find fixed numbers $a$ and $b$ — read
straight off that known distribution — that trap $Q$ with the desired probability:
$$
P\bigl(a \le Q \le b\bigr) = 1-\alpha .
$$
This is a true probability statement that requires no knowledge of $\theta$, precisely
because $Q$'s distribution does not depend on $\theta$. Now comes the move that turns it
into an interval: if we can **algebraically rearrange** the double inequality
$a \le Q \le b$ so that $\theta$ sits alone in the middle,
$$
a \le Q(X_1,\dots,X_n;\theta) \le b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
L(X_1,\dots,X_n) \le \theta \le U(X_1,\dots,X_n),
$$
then the *same probability* transfers to the rearranged event, because the two events are
**identical** — every sample making one true makes the other true. Therefore
$$
P\bigl(L \le \theta \le U\bigr) = P\bigl(a\le Q\le b\bigr) = 1-\alpha,
$$
which is exactly @def-ci. The endpoints $L,U$ came out of the rearrangement, so they are
functions of the data alone — no $\theta$ left in them, which is mandatory, since you
cannot report an interval whose endpoints depend on the unknown you are estimating.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why must the pivot's distribution not depend on $\theta$?"* Because the numbers $a,b$ are
chosen from that distribution *before* we know $\theta$. If the distribution shifted with
$\theta$, the cutoffs that trap $Q$ with probability $1-\alpha$ would themselves depend on
the unknown, and we could not compute them. The "distribution-free-of-$\theta$" requirement
is exactly what lets us pick $a,b$ once and have the coverage hold for *every* $\theta$ — the
"for every $\theta$" clause in @def-ci. This is the structural reason the t-statistic (next
article) is built as a *ratio* that cancels the unknown $\sigma$: cancelling unknowns is how
you manufacture a pivot.
:::

## Building the canonical interval: a Normal mean, known variance {#sec-build}

Now we run the machine on the case that started it all. Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. with
unknown mean $\mu$ and **known** variance $\sigma^2$. (Known variance is the honest
simplification that isolates the *idea*; INFR-C2 removes it, and the removal is exactly
what forces the t-distribution. We flag the assumption loudly rather than hide it.)

**Step 1 — find a pivot.** From INFR-A1, the sample mean $\bar X = \frac1n\sum X_i$ has
$\mathbb E[\bar X]=\mu$ and $\operatorname{Var}(\bar X)=\sigma^2/n$. Standardize it:
$$
Z \;=\; \frac{\bar X - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-pivot}
Is this a pivot? It is a function of the data ($\bar X$) and the parameter ($\mu$). Its
distribution: if the $X_i$ are themselves Normal, then $\bar X$ is exactly Normal and $Z$
is exactly **standard Normal** $\mathcal N(0,1)$; if the $X_i$ are not Normal, the **Central
Limit Theorem** from INFR-A2 says $Z \xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$, so $Z$ is
*approximately* standard Normal for large $n$. Either way the distribution of $Z$ — standard
Normal — contains **no unknown**: $\mu$ has been subtracted off and $\sigma$ is known. So
$Z$ is a (exact or asymptotic) pivot. This is the precise point where the whole track's
foundation pays off: *the CLT is what makes $Z$ a pivot when the data are not Normal.*

**Step 2 — trap the pivot.** We want the central chunk holding probability $1-\alpha$,
leaving $\alpha/2$ in each tail. Define $z_{\alpha/2}$ as the value with upper-tail area
$\alpha/2$, i.e. $P(Z > z_{\alpha/2}) = \alpha/2$. (These quantiles are tabulated and built
in [PROB-11]; for $\alpha=0.05$, $z_{0.025}=1.959963\ldots\approx 1.96$.)

First note the standard-Normal density is **even**: writing $\varphi(t) =
e^{-t^2/2}/\sqrt{2\pi}$, replacing $t$ by $-t$ leaves $(-t)^2=t^2$ unchanged, so
$\varphi(-t)=\varphi(t)$ — the curve is its own mirror image across $0$. Integrating this
mirror symmetry, the area in the left tail below $-z_{\alpha/2}$ equals the area in the
right tail above $+z_{\alpha/2}$:
$$
P\bigl(Z < -z_{\alpha/2}\bigr)
= \int_{-\infty}^{-z_{\alpha/2}}\!\varphi(t)\,dt
\;\overset{t\to -t}{=}\;
\int_{z_{\alpha/2}}^{\infty}\!\varphi(t)\,dt
= P\bigl(Z > z_{\alpha/2}\bigr) = \frac{\alpha}{2},
$$
where the middle step substitutes $t \mapsto -t$ (which flips the limits and, via
$\varphi(-t)=\varphi(t)$, leaves the integrand alone). So the lower cutoff is
$-z_{\alpha/2}$, and the two tails together carry $\alpha/2+\alpha/2=\alpha$. The total
probability is $1$, so the central region keeps the rest:
$$
P\bigl(-z_{\alpha/2} \le Z \le z_{\alpha/2}\bigr)
= 1 - P\bigl(Z < -z_{\alpha/2}\bigr) - P\bigl(Z > z_{\alpha/2}\bigr)
= 1 - \tfrac{\alpha}{2} - \tfrac{\alpha}{2}
= 1-\alpha.
$$ {#eq-trap}
@fig-pivot-tails draws this: the central shaded mass is $1-\alpha$, the two tails are
$\alpha/2$ each.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pivot-tails
#| fig-cap: 'Trapping the pivot. The standard Normal density of $Z=(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$. The central shaded region between $-z_{\alpha/2}$ and $+z_{\alpha/2}$ holds probability $1-\alpha=0.95$; each unshaded tail holds $\alpha/2=0.025$. The cutoff $z_{0.025}=1.96$ is read straight off this fixed, known curve — it does not depend on the unknown $\mu$, which is exactly what makes the construction work.'
zc = 1.96
t = np.linspace(-4, 4, 600)
pdf = normal_pdf(t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(t, pdf, color=TRUTH)
mask = (t >= -zc) & (t <= zc)
ax.fill_between(t[mask], pdf[mask], color=FILL, alpha=0.7,
                label=r"central mass $= 1-\alpha = 0.95$")
ax.fill_between(t[t <= -zc], pdf[t <= -zc], color=MISS, alpha=0.6)
ax.fill_between(t[t >= zc], pdf[t >= zc], color=MISS, alpha=0.6,
                label=r"each tail $= \alpha/2 = 0.025$")
for s in (-zc, zc):
    ax.axvline(s, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
ax.text(zc + 0.05, 0.05, r"$+z_{\alpha/2}=1.96$", fontsize=9)
ax.text(-zc - 0.05, 0.05, r"$-z_{\alpha/2}$", fontsize=9, ha="right")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$Z = (\bar X - \mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt{n})$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("The pivot's known distribution sets the cutoffs")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**Step 3 — rearrange to isolate $\mu$.** This is pure algebra on the inequality inside
@eq-trap, and we show every step because the rearrangement is where students lose the
thread. Substitute the pivot @eq-pivot:
$$
-z_{\alpha/2} \;\le\; \frac{\bar X - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \;\le\; z_{\alpha/2}.
$$
Multiply all three parts by $\sigma/\sqrt n$, which is a **positive** number (it is a
standard deviation), so the inequality directions are preserved:
$$
-z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\; \bar X - \mu \;\le\;
z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$
Subtract $\bar X$ from all three parts:
$$
-\bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\; -\mu \;\le\;
-\bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$
Multiply through by $-1$. **Multiplying by a negative number flips both inequality signs**
— the single step everyone forgets — so the chain reverses:
$$
\bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\ge\; \mu \;\ge\;
\bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$
Read right-to-left to put it in increasing order:
$$
\bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\; \mu \;\le\;
\bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-rearranged}

The event in @eq-rearranged is *the very same event* as the one in @eq-trap — we only did
reversible algebra, so a sample satisfies one exactly when it satisfies the other.
Therefore they carry the identical probability $1-\alpha$. Matching @eq-rearranged against
@def-ci, we read off the endpoints:
$$
\boxed{\;
L = \bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n},
\qquad
U = \bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n},
\;}
$$ {#eq-ci-mean}
i.e. the interval $\bar X \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. We have proved:

::: {#thm-ci-mean}
**Theorem (CI for a Normal mean, known variance).** If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are i.i.d.
$\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, then
$$
\Bigl[\;\bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\tfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt n},\;\;
\bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\tfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}\;\Bigr]
$$
is an *exact* $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\mu$. If the $X_i$ are not Normal but
have finite variance, the same interval has coverage $\to 1-\alpha$ as $n\to\infty$ by the
CLT (an *approximate* interval).
:::

::: {.proof}
The pivot $Z=(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ is standard Normal (exactly, under Normality of
the $X_i$, since a linear combination of independent Normals is Normal — established in the
Probability track; asymptotically otherwise, by the CLT of INFR-A2). Equation @eq-trap
holds by the definition of $z_{\alpha/2}$ and the symmetry of the standard Normal. The chain
@eq-pivot–@eq-rearranged is a sequence of reversible algebraic operations (multiply by a
positive constant, subtract a constant, multiply by $-1$ flipping the signs), so the event
$\{-z_{\alpha/2}\le Z\le z_{\alpha/2}\}$ equals the event $\{L\le\mu\le U\}$ pointwise in the
sample space. Equal events have equal probability, hence $P(L\le\mu\le U)=1-\alpha$, which is
@def-ci. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Read the formula for its meaning, the same way we read the OLS slope. The interval is
**centered at the estimate** $\bar X$ (your best guess sits in the middle), and its
**half-width** is $z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\mathrm{SE}$ — the standard error scaled by how much
confidence you demanded. Three levers set the width, and each makes intuitive sense:

- **More confidence widens it.** Larger $1-\alpha$ means smaller tails, larger
  $z_{\alpha/2}$, wider interval. To be right more often you must claim a bigger net. ($95\%
  \to 1.96$, $99\%\to 2.576$.)
- **More data narrows it**, like $1/\sqrt n$ through the standard error — the same
  $\sqrt n$ from the CLT. Quadrupling the sample halves the width.
- **More noise widens it**, linearly in $\sigma$. A noisier population needs a bigger net
  for the same reliability.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why $z_{\alpha/2}$ and not $z_\alpha$?"* Because we split the missing $\alpha$ probability
*equally* between the two tails — $\alpha/2$ above and $\alpha/2$ below — to get the
shortest symmetric interval for a symmetric distribution. Putting all $\alpha$ in one tail
gives a valid *one-sided* interval (a confidence bound), e.g. "$\mu \ge \bar X - z_\alpha\,
\sigma/\sqrt n$ with $95\%$ confidence," which is the right tool when you only care about
one direction (a lower bound on a safety margin, an upper bound on a defect rate). Two-sided
with equal tails is the default because it is shortest when the pivot is symmetric and we
have no reason to favor a side.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

## Example 1 — calibrating a scale, by hand {#sec-scale}

A lab calibrates a digital scale by weighing a certified $100.0$ g reference mass $n=12$
times. The measurement noise is known from the manufacturer's spec to have standard
deviation $\sigma=0.8$ g (this is the genuine "known variance" regime — the instrument's
precision is a documented constant, while the *true* reading $\mu$, possibly biased away
from $100$, is what we estimate). We carry every number in the open.

```{python}
#| label: ex-scale
#| echo: true
sigma_scale = 0.8
n_scale = 12
z95 = 1.959963984540054   # z_{0.025}, the exact 95% two-sided quantile
readings = np.array([100.3, 99.6, 100.9, 100.1, 99.8, 100.5,
                     100.2, 99.9, 100.7, 100.0, 100.4, 100.2])
xbar = readings.mean()
se = sigma_scale / np.sqrt(n_scale)
half = z95 * se
lo, hi = xbar - half, xbar + half
print(f"n            = {n_scale}")
print(f"sample mean  xbar = {xbar:.4f} g")
print(f"std error    SE   = sigma/sqrt(n) = {sigma_scale}/sqrt({n_scale}) = {se:.4f} g")
print(f"half-width   z*SE = 1.95996 * {se:.4f} = {half:.4f} g")
print(f"95% CI for mu     = [{lo:.4f}, {hi:.4f}] g")
```

Walk the arithmetic against @eq-ci-mean:

- The estimate is $\bar X = `{python} f"{xbar:.3f}"`$ g — the scale reads slightly high on
  average.
- The standard error is $\mathrm{SE}=\sigma/\sqrt n = 0.8/\sqrt{12} =
  `{python} f"{se:.4f}"`$ g.
- The half-width is $z_{0.025}\cdot\mathrm{SE} = 1.96\times `{python} f"{se:.4f}"` =
  `{python} f"{half:.4f}"`$ g.
- So the $95\%$ interval is $`{python} f"{xbar:.3f}"` \pm `{python} f"{half:.4f}"` =
  [`{python} f"{lo:.3f}"`,\, `{python} f"{hi:.3f}"`]$ g.

The honest reading: $100.0$ g lies inside this interval, so the calibration data are
**consistent with** the scale being unbiased at the $95\%$ level — we have not detected a
bias. We have *not* shown the scale is perfectly calibrated; we have shown the data do not
rule out $100.0$. (That "fail to reject" framing is the bridge to hypothesis testing,
INFR-D1, where we prove the interval and the test are two views of one thing.)

## Example 2 — does the interval actually cover? A simulation {#sec-sim}

The coverage claim is testable, and we should test it rather than trust it. We play
$10{,}000$ parallel universes: in each, draw a fresh sample from a *known* truth, build the
$95\%$ interval, and count how often it caught the true mean. We run three regimes that
isolate the two assumptions behind @thm-ci-mean — Normality of the pivot, and known
variance:

1. **Normal data, known $\sigma$** — both assumptions hold, so the interval is *exact*.
2. **Skewed (Exponential) data, known $\sigma$** — Normality of the pivot is only
   *asymptotic* (it leans on the CLT), but the variance is still genuinely known.
3. **Skewed data, $\sigma$ *estimated*** — the realistic regime, where we plug the sample
   standard deviation into the standard error. This is *outside* @thm-ci-mean and is exactly
   what INFR-C2 will fix; we include it to show what breaks.

```{python}
#| label: fig-coverage-sim
#| fig-cap: 'Empirical coverage over 10,000 simulated studies, dissecting the two assumptions. **Left, known $\sigma$:** with the true variance plugged in, coverage stays close to nominal 95% (dashed) for both Normal data (exact, flat at 95%) and Exponential data (the skew costs almost nothing here, since the only error is the pivot''s non-Normality, which the CLT controls). **Right, estimated $\sigma$:** the *realistic* case — plugging the sample SD into a z-interval *undercovers* badly at small $n$ (around 81% at $n=5$), because a noisy variance estimate makes the symmetric Normal cutoffs too tight. This is the gap INFR-C2 closes with Student''s t. The honest lesson: it is the *unknown variance*, more than mild skew, that breaks the small-sample z-interval.'
def coverage(mode, n, mu_true, sigma_true, reps=10000):
    """mode in {'normal_known', 'exp_known', 'exp_estimated'}."""
    z = 1.959963984540054
    hits = 0
    for _ in range(reps):
        if mode == "normal_known":
            s = rng.normal(mu_true, sigma_true, size=n)
            half = z * sigma_true / np.sqrt(n)
        elif mode == "exp_known":
            s = rng.exponential(mu_true, size=n)   # mean = sd = mu_true
            half = z * sigma_true / np.sqrt(n)
        else:  # exp_estimated: plug in the sample SD (the realistic z-interval)
            s = rng.exponential(mu_true, size=n)
            half = z * s.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n)
        xbar = s.mean()
        if xbar - half <= mu_true <= xbar + half:
            hits += 1
    return hits / reps

ns = [5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160]
cov_normal = [coverage("normal_known", n, 10.0, 4.0) for n in ns]
cov_exp_kn = [coverage("exp_known", n, 10.0, 10.0) for n in ns]
cov_exp_es = [coverage("exp_estimated", n, 10.0, 10.0) for n in ns]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8), sharey=True)
axL.axhline(0.95, color=TRUTH, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="nominal 95%")
axL.plot(ns, cov_normal, "o-", color=DATA, label="Normal (known $\\sigma$)")
axL.plot(ns, cov_exp_kn, "s-", color=ACCENT, label="Exponential (known $\\sigma$)")
axL.set_title("Known variance: both fine")
axL.set_ylabel("empirical coverage")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0.95, color=TRUTH, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="nominal 95%")
axR.plot(ns, cov_exp_es, "D-", color=MISS, label="Exponential (estimated $\\sigma$)")
axR.set_title("Estimated variance: undercovers")
axR.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_xticks(ns); ax.set_xticklabels(ns)
    ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylim(0.84, 0.98)
plt.tight_layout()
print("Normal,      known sigma:", [f"{c:.3f}" for c in cov_normal])
print("Exponential, known sigma:", [f"{c:.3f}" for c in cov_exp_kn])
print("Exponential, est.  sigma:", [f"{c:.3f}" for c in cov_exp_es])
```

The left panel is the theory keeping its promise. Under Normality the pivot is *exactly*
standard Normal, so coverage hugs $95\%$ at every $n$, even $n=5$. For skewed Exponential
data *with the variance still known*, coverage also stays close to $95\%$ — the only
imperfection is that the pivot $Z$ is not quite Normal at small $n$, and the CLT keeps that
error small. So, perhaps surprisingly, **mild skew alone barely dents the known-variance
interval.**

The right panel is where the wheels come off, and it is the realistic case: we no longer
know $\sigma$, so we plug in the sample standard deviation. Now coverage collapses to around
$81\%$ at $n=5$ and is still only $\approx 87\%$ at $n=10$, climbing back toward $95\%$ only
as $n$ grows into the dozens. The culprit is not the
skew — it is that a *noisy estimate of the variance* makes the symmetric cutoffs
$\pm 1.96$ too tight: when the sample happens to underestimate $\sigma$, the interval is too
narrow and misses more often than $5\%$ of the time. This is precisely the failure that
**Student's t-distribution** repairs by widening the cutoffs to account for the extra
uncertainty in $\hat\sigma$ — the entire subject of [INFR-C2]. The honest lesson, and the
one this simulation was built to teach: the assumption that quietly matters most for a
small-sample interval is **known variance**, not Normality.

## Example 3 — a polling proportion at the edge of applicability {#sec-poll}

A pollster surveys $n=1000$ likely voters and finds $\hat p = 0.52$ supporting a measure.
A proportion is a mean of $0/1$ indicators, so the same machine applies, with one wrinkle
that makes it an honest *edge* case: **the variance is not separately known** — for a
Bernoulli$(p)$ the variance is $p(1-p)$, which depends on the very unknown we are
estimating. The textbook move is to *plug in* $\hat p$ for $p$ in the standard error, giving
the **Wald interval** $\hat p \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$. This is no longer
strictly the known-variance case of @thm-ci-mean — it is an approximation on top of an
approximation — and it is famous for behaving badly when $p$ is near $0$ or $1$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-poll
#| fig-cap: 'A proportion is a mean of 0/1 indicators, so the z-interval machine applies — but the variance $p(1-p)$ must be estimated, making it an approximation. **Left:** the Wald 95% interval for the poll, $\hat p=0.52$, $n=1000$. **Right:** the edge of applicability — empirical coverage of the Wald interval (red) as the true $p$ approaches the boundary, with $n=40$. Near $p=0.05$ or $p=0.95$ the Wald interval badly *undercovers* (well below nominal 95%), because the Normal approximation to a near-degenerate binomial fails. The Wilson interval (green) repairs it. This failure is the launch point for INFR-C3.'
# Left: the poll's Wald interval.
phat, n_poll = 0.52, 1000
se_p = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat) / n_poll)
half_p = 1.959963984540054 * se_p
lo_p, hi_p = phat - half_p, phat + half_p

# Right: Wald vs Wilson coverage as p approaches the boundary, n = 40.
def wald_wilson_coverage(p, n=40, reps=4000):
    z = 1.959963984540054
    wald_hits, wilson_hits = 0, 0
    for _ in range(reps):
        x = rng.binomial(n, p)
        ph = x / n
        # Wald
        h = z * np.sqrt(ph * (1 - ph) / n)
        if ph - h <= p <= ph + h:
            wald_hits += 1
        # Wilson
        denom = 1 + z**2 / n
        center = (ph + z**2 / (2 * n)) / denom
        margin = (z / denom) * np.sqrt(ph * (1 - ph) / n + z**2 / (4 * n**2))
        if center - margin <= p <= center + margin:
            wilson_hits += 1
    return wald_hits / reps, wilson_hits / reps

ps = np.array([0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.35, 0.50])
wald_cov, wilson_cov = zip(*[wald_wilson_coverage(p) for p in ps])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.errorbar([0], [phat], yerr=[[half_p], [half_p]], fmt="o", color=DATA,
             capsize=6, ms=8, lw=2)
axL.axhline(0.5, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2, label="p = 0.50 (a tie)")
axL.set_xlim(-1, 1); axL.set_xticks([])
axL.set_ylabel("support proportion")
axL.set_title(fr"Wald 95% CI: [{lo_p:.3f}, {hi_p:.3f}]")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0.95, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="nominal 95%")
axR.plot(ps, wald_cov, "o-", color=TRUTH, label="Wald")
axR.plot(ps, wilson_cov, "s-", color=ACCENT, label="Wilson")
axR.set_xlabel("true proportion $p$")
axR.set_ylabel("empirical coverage")
axR.set_title("Edge of applicability ($n=40$)")
axR.set_ylim(0.80, 1.0)
axR.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"Poll Wald 95% CI for support: [{lo_p:.4f}, {hi_p:.4f}]")
print(f"Wald coverage by p:   {[f'{c:.3f}' for c in wald_cov]}")
print(f"Wilson coverage by p: {[f'{c:.3f}' for c in wilson_cov]}")
```

For the headline poll ($\hat p=0.52$, $n=1000$), the interval is $[`{python}
f"{lo_p:.3f}"`,\, `{python} f"{hi_p:.3f}"`]$ — comfortably straddling $0.50$, so the data do
*not* establish majority support: a tie is inside the interval. (The "$\pm 3$ points margin
of error" you hear on the news is exactly $z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}\approx
`{python} f"{half_p:.3f}"`$, computed at $\hat p=0.5$ where it is widest.)

The right panel is the honest boundary. With a small sample ($n=40$) and a true $p$ near
$0.05$, the Wald interval's *actual* coverage collapses well below the advertised $95\%$ —
sometimes catastrophically, because a near-degenerate binomial is poorly approximated by a
Normal, and the plug-in $\hat p(1-\hat p)$ can be wildly off (it is literally $0$ when the
sample happens to be all-$0$ or all-$1$, producing a zero-width interval that covers
nothing). **This is the edge of applicability of the basic z/Wald interval**, and the fix is
to use a better pivot — the **Wilson** interval (green, which inverts a test rather than
plugging in) or **Agresti–Coull** — both of which restore coverage near the boundary. That
repair is the explicit subject of [INFR-C3], which builds Wald, score, and profile intervals
and shows exactly why the score-based Wilson interval wins here.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The z-interval assumes either Normal data or a large enough $n$ for the CLT — and known
variance.** The proportion example violated "known variance" (we plugged in $\hat p$), and
the simulation showed coverage degrading near the boundary and at small $n$. Two general
lessons: (1) when $\sigma$ must be *estimated*, the correct pivot is no longer Normal but
**Student's t** — the entire point of [INFR-C2]; (2) when the sampling distribution is
skewed or the parameter is near a boundary, the symmetric $\pm z\cdot\mathrm{SE}$ form
under-covers, and you reach for a better pivot (Wilson/score, [INFR-C3]) or an
assumption-light method (the **bootstrap**, [INFR-E2]). The formula @eq-ci-mean is correct
*given its assumptions*; honesty is knowing when they hold.
:::

# What a confidence interval is *not*: the four classic traps {#sec-misinterpret}

This is the interview-and-exam heart of the article. Each misreading below is one a sharp
examiner will dangle in front of you; each is a failure to keep straight *where the
randomness lives* (@sec-definition). We name the trap, state the correct reading, and say
why.

**Trap 1 — "There is a $95\%$ probability that $\mu$ lies in *this* interval $[9.1,
11.3]$."** This is the cardinal error. Once computed, $[9.1, 11.3]$ is a fixed pair of
numbers and $\mu$ is a fixed number; the statement "$9.1\le\mu\le 11.3$" is simply true or
false. There is no random quantity left to carry a probability. **Correct reading:** the
*procedure* that produced these endpoints captures $\mu$ in $95\%$ of repetitions; this
particular interval is one realization, either right or wrong. The probability lived in
$L,U$ *before* you plugged in data, and you spent it.

**Trap 2 — "$95\%$ of the data / of future observations fall in the interval."** No — the
interval is about the *parameter* $\mu$ (a property of the population's center), not about
individual data points. A $95\%$ CI for the mean is typically *far narrower* than the spread
of the data, and shrinks to a point as $n\to\infty$, whereas $95\%$ of the data occupy a
fixed-width band no matter how much data you collect. The object that contains $95\%$ of
*future observations* is a **prediction interval**, a different and wider thing (it must
account for the new point's own noise, not just the uncertainty in $\bar X$).
@fig-ci-vs-pi makes the gap vivid.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ci-vs-pi
#| fig-cap: 'Trap 2, defused. For $n=200$ draws from $\mathcal N(50, 8^2)$: the **confidence interval for the mean** (green, narrow) pins down where the *center* is and is much narrower than the data spread; the **prediction interval** (orange, wide) is where a *new observation* would land and roughly matches the data''s $\pm 2\sigma$ scatter. They answer different questions. As $n$ grows the CI shrinks toward a point while the PI converges to a fixed width — a confidence interval is not a range for the data.'
mu_d, sigma_d, n_d = 50.0, 8.0, 200
data = rng.normal(mu_d, sigma_d, size=n_d)
xbar_d = data.mean()
se_d = sigma_d / np.sqrt(n_d)
z = 1.959963984540054
ci = (xbar_d - z * se_d, xbar_d + z * se_d)
# Prediction interval (known sigma): xbar +/- z * sigma * sqrt(1 + 1/n).
pi_half = z * sigma_d * np.sqrt(1 + 1 / n_d)
pi = (xbar_d - pi_half, xbar_d + pi_half)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
ax.hist(data, bins=24, color=FILL, alpha=0.7, label="observed data")
ax.axvspan(ci[0], ci[1], color=ACCENT, alpha=0.5,
           label=f"95% CI for mean (width {ci[1]-ci[0]:.2f})")
ax.axvspan(pi[0], pi[1], color=MISS, alpha=0.25,
           label=f"95% prediction interval (width {pi[1]-pi[0]:.1f})")
ax.axvline(xbar_d, color=TRUTH, lw=1.6)
ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.set_title("A CI for the mean is not a range for the data")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"CI for mean:        [{ci[0]:.2f}, {ci[1]:.2f}]   width {ci[1]-ci[0]:.2f}")
print(f"Prediction interval:[{pi[0]:.2f}, {pi[1]:.2f}]  width {pi[1]-pi[0]:.2f}")
```

**Trap 3 — "A $95\%$ CI means $95\%$ of *all my study's* parameters are in their
intervals" / confusing it with replication probability.** The coverage is per-interval over
hypothetical repetitions of *the same* experiment, not a tally across different parameters
in one study, and it is **not** the probability that a replication's estimate lands inside
your interval (that probability is different and generally lower). The interval says nothing
direct about what the *next study* will find; it says the *method* traps the *fixed truth*
$95\%$ of the time.

**Trap 4 — "A wider interval means a worse / less confident result."** Width and confidence
are different axes. At a *fixed* level $1-\alpha$, a wide interval means an *imprecise*
estimate (small $n$ or large $\sigma$), not a less reliable procedure — the coverage is
still $95\%$. Conversely you can always make an interval narrower by *lowering* the
confidence level, which is not "a better result," just a weaker promise. The honest summary
of a study reports the interval *and* its level; quoting a narrow interval without its level
is meaningless.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me the one-sentence correct interpretation of a $95\%$ confidence interval."* — *"It
is one realization of a procedure that, used repeatedly on fresh samples, would contain the
true parameter $95\%$ of the time."* If pressed for the contrast: *"It is not a $95\%$
probability statement about this particular interval — that is the Bayesian **credible
interval**, which requires a prior and treats the parameter as random ([INFR-E4])."* Being
able to say both the correct frequentist reading *and* name the Bayesian object that *does*
license the tempting interpretation is exactly the depth an interviewer is checking for.
:::

# The duality you will keep meeting {#sec-duality}

One structural fact is worth planting now because it recurs through the whole testing
movement (D): **a confidence interval and a hypothesis test are two views of the same
machine.** The $(1-\alpha)$ interval for $\mu$ is *exactly the set of null values
$\mu_0$ that a level-$\alpha$ two-sided test would not reject.* This is not a coincidence to
take on faith — it is the *same algebra* we already did in Step 3, read in the other
direction, so we do it here in full.

A level-$\alpha$ two-sided test of $H_0:\mu=\mu_0$ uses the test statistic
$Z_0 = (\bar X - \mu_0)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ — note this is the pivot @eq-pivot with the
*hypothesized* value $\mu_0$ in place of the unknown $\mu$, so under $H_0$ it is standard
Normal. The test **fails to reject** $H_0$ exactly when $Z_0$ lands in the same central
region @eq-trap that we used to build the interval:
$$
-z_{\alpha/2} \;\le\; \frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \;\le\; z_{\alpha/2}.
$$
But this is character-for-character the inequality at the top of Step 3, with $\mu_0$ where
$\mu$ stood. So we may rerun the *identical* reversible algebra — multiply by the positive
$\sigma/\sqrt n$, subtract $\bar X$, multiply by $-1$ and flip — and it lands at the same
place @eq-rearranged:
$$
\bar X - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\; \mu_0 \;\le\;
\bar X + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$
The left side is "the test does not reject $\mu_0$"; the right side is "$\mu_0$ lies in our
interval @eq-ci-mean." They are the same set of $\mu_0$. So "is $\mu_0$ in the $95\%$ CI?"
and "does the level-$0.05$ test reject $\mu_0$?" are the same question. This is why, in
Example 1, "$100$ g is inside the interval" was the same statement as "we fail to reject
that the scale is unbiased." We prove this duality at full generality — for any pivot, not
just this one — and use it relentlessly in [INFR-D1].

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the Foundations movement: **[INFR-A1]** made the estimator a random
variable with a sampling distribution and a standard error, and **[INFR-A2]** pinned the
size ($\sigma/\sqrt n$) and shape (Gaussian, via the CLT) of that distribution. This article
turned those into the first *interval* and, crucially, fixed what the interval claims. From
here the Interval movement continues:

- **[INFR-C2 *The t-Distribution and Small-Sample Intervals*]** — drops the artificial
  "known $\sigma$" assumption of @thm-ci-mean. Plugging in an estimate $\hat\sigma$ breaks
  the Normal pivot; the repaired pivot follows **Student's t**, and we build it (and the
  chi-square distribution of the sample variance) from scratch. This is the interval you
  actually use in practice.
- **[INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]** — generalizes the pivot machine to
  *any* MLE-estimated parameter, and fixes the proportion failure of @sec-poll with the
  Wilson/score interval.
- **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** — collects the duality of @sec-duality and
  makes the test/interval correspondence a theorem.
- **[INFR-E2 *Bootstrap Confidence Intervals*]** — builds intervals by resampling when no
  clean pivot exists, the assumption-light alternative previewed in the Pitfall.
- **[INFR-E4 *The Bayesian Turn*]** — the deliberate foil: the **credible interval**, which
  *does* put $95\%$ probability on the parameter, and the philosophical price of doing so.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding source.** J. Neyman, "Outline of a Theory of Statistical Estimation Based
  on the Classical Theory of Probability," *Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A* 236 (1937): the paper
  that defined confidence intervals as a property of the procedure and insisted, against the
  Bayesian reading, that no probability attaches to the individual interval.
- **The proportion disaster, in full.** L. D. Brown, T. T. Cai, A. DasGupta, "Interval
  Estimation for a Binomial Proportion," *Statistical Science* 16 (2001): the definitive
  account of why the Wald interval of @sec-poll fails and why the Wilson/score interval is
  the right default — read it before [INFR-C3].
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *What if $\sigma$ is unknown?* — the t-interval,
  [INFR-C2]. *What if there is no clean pivot at all?* — the bootstrap, [INFR-E1]–[INFR-E2].
  *Can I legitimately say "$95\%$ probability $\mu$ is in here"?* — only under a prior, and
  then it is a credible interval, [INFR-E4]. *Is the equal-tailed interval optimal?* — for a
  symmetric pivot it is shortest; for skewed pivots the shortest interval is *not*
  equal-tailed, a subtlety [INFR-C3] takes up.
